text: All performance levels of marathon and half-marathon runners use positive pacing strategies (decreasing speed over the race), regardless of sex.
Higher-performing male marathon runners show significantly more even pacing with an absolute change of speed of 4.14%, compared to 7.9% for low-level male marathon runners.
Higher-performing male half-marathon runners show an absolute change of speed of 2.97%, compared to 6.05% for low-level male half-marathon runners.
Higher-performing female marathon runners show an absolute change of speed of 3.16%, compared to 5.83% for low-level female marathon runners.
Half-marathon runners demonstrate more even pacing than marathon runners across all performance groups, though the practical significance is trivial in women.
Better runners maintain more even pacing throughout long-distance races, suggesting that minimizing speed variation is associated with superior performance.

The difference in pacing evenness between marathon and half-marathon is likely driven by carbohydrate availability, as carbohydrate use in metabolic processes increases supralinearly with running intensity.
